summary: Dan Shapiro answers a reader's question about why her planer keeps tearing chunks from the corners of boards during final thickness passes. He offers two theories: moisture absorption at board ends weakening the wood, and the possibility of heavily figured wood causing tearout. He provides practical solutions including using sacrificial scrap lumber, checking blade sharpness, and avoiding thick passes. The post showcases Shapiro's self-deprecating humor, admitting to only four months of planer ownership while confidently diagnosing the problem.
key_points: Lumber absorbs moisture faster at the ends, potentially weakening those areas and making them prone to chunking during planing
Figured wood with undulating grain creates two cut points per pass, leading to tearout that worsens as boards get thinner
Attaching sacrificial scrap lumber to the back of boards can protect valuable wood from end-grain damage
Dull blades exacerbate tearout by 'whacking more and cutting less'
money_quotes: As someone who's owned a planer for nearly four months and planed literally dozens of board-feet of lumber, I feel fully qualified to answer your question.
By the time you're at the end of the board, you don't have half a tree of lumber behind you to hold the whole thing in place.
In figured wood the grain looks like misbehaving spaghetti; it actually undulates back and forth.
Your gorgeous piece of curly lumber starts resembling a lunar landscape after a (very noisy) pass through the machine.
Dull blades will cause/exacerbate both of these problems, since you're whacking more and cutting less.
Your questions are welcome, as long as you don't mind an incorrect answer.
key_insight: Chunking at board ends during final planer passes likely results from moisture-weakened end grain or figured wood tearout, both solvable with sacrificial backing boards and sharp blades.
